refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; log all link transitions; calculate availability = (total_time - downtime) / total_time; compare against 0.999 threshold. Acceptance requires independent verification by Safety Manager and Site Manager.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and initiate safe failsafe action within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link quality metrics (signal strength, latency, packet loss) to the tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.5: The network infrastructure shall support redundant communication paths to achieve ≥0.999 availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover and link quality monitoring
DP-C: Mesh network topology with multi-hop routing and dynamic path selection
DP-D: Satellite + terrestrial hybrid link with seamless switchover based on signal quality thresholds
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover and link quality monitoring. Primary path uses licensed spectrum (e.g., LTE/5G); backup path uses alternative spectrum (e.g., private radio or satellite). Automatic failover triggered when primary link quality degrades below threshold (e.g., RSRP < -120 dBm or latency > 500 ms). Link quality metrics continuously reported via Telemetry interface.

potential_sources: Coupling between link failover logic and vehicle safety state machine (must ensure safe state during switchover)
Coupling between network infrastructure (DP-B realization) and tele-operator UI (must display link status clearly)
Coupling between backup link provisioning cost and project budget constraints
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is decoupled from vehicle propulsion/control logic; failover is transparent to operator commands. Coupling is manageable via well-defined Tasking and Telemetry interfaces. Safety criticality (high) justifies dual-path investment.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Two independent communication paths (different spectrum/providers) are available at the worksite.
ASSUMPTION: Automatic failover latency (< 100 ms) is achievable with selected network technologies.
RISK: Backup link may have higher latency or lower bandwidth than primary; operator experience may degrade during failover.
RISK: Dual-path infrastructure cost may exceed budget; customer may demand cost-benefit trade-off analysis.
RISK: Regulatory approval for dual spectrum use (licensed + private) may introduce delays.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to explicitly specify: (1) primary/backup spectrum choices (e.g., LTE Cat-M1 + LoRaWAN), (2) failover decision criteria (signal quality thresholds, latency limits), (3) switchover latency budget (target ≤ 100 ms), (4) link quality metrics to be reported (RSRP, latency, packet loss %). Add cost-benefit analysis comparing DP-B vs. DP-C (mesh) for customer decision gate.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-path communication architecture, link failover logic, link quality monitoring, and tele-operator-facing link status reporting. Includes command uplink processing, telemetry downlink aggregation, and failover state machine. Does NOT own vehicle control logic (owned by Vehicle module) or safety policy enforcement (owned by Safety Services module).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (e.g., move forward, rotate, stop) from tele-operator console; Teleoperation module queues commands for transmission over primary/backup links.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings) and link quality metrics (RSRP, latency, packet loss, link status: up/down) to tele-operator console and Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints from Safety Services (e.g., geofence, speed limits); sends link loss alerts to Safety Services to trigger failsafe actions (e.g., emergency stop).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts primary and backup communication paths; Teleoperation module sends commands and receives telemetry via Network Link; Network Link handles failover logic and reports link quality.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Teleoperation module is the natural owner of link availability, failover logic, and link quality monitoring. All sub-FRs (010.1-010.5) map directly to Teleoperation responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface (safety policy) is external.

Poor fit. Vehicle module owns propulsion, steering, and onboard sensors. Link availability is a communication concern, not a vehicle subsystem concern. Assigning to Vehicle would diffuse communication logic across vehicle firmware and create tight coupling with vehicle control.

Poor fit. Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement (geofence, speed limits, emergency stop). Link availability is a prerequisite for safe teleoperation, but the mechanism (dual paths, failover) is a communication architecture concern, not a safety policy concern. Assigning to Safety Services would conflate communication infrastructure with safety logic.
Poor fit. NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning link availability to NGHE Solution would leave implementation responsibility undefined and diffuse across multiple sub-modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the single, cohesive owner of teleoperation link availability. It encapsulates dual-path communication, failover logic, and link quality monitoring. It interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert) but maintains clear separation of concerns: Teleoperation handles 'how to communicate reliably,' Safety Services handles 'what to do if communication fails.'
containment_risks: RISK: If backup link provisioning (e.g., satellite service contract) is managed by a separate team, ownership clarity may blur. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contract for Network Link; Teleoperation owns failover logic, Network Link owner manages dual-path provisioning.

RISK: If vehicle firmware must implement link loss detection (e.g., watchdog timer), responsibility may split between Teleoperation and Vehicle. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert as the signal from Teleoperation to Vehicle; Vehicle responds to SafetyAlert but does not own link monitoring.

RISK: Tele-operator console (UI) must display link status; if console is owned by a separate team, interface clarity is critical. Mitigation: Telemetry interface explicitly includes link quality metrics; console owner consumes Telemetry and renders UI.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Network Link (backup communication infrastructure) is provisioned and owned by a dedicated network/infrastructure team, with Teleoperation module consuming it via a well-defined interface contract. Establish a design review gate to verify that failover logic (Teleoperation) and failsafe action (Safety Services) are synchronized: when Teleoperation detects link loss, it must immediately signal SafetyAlert to Safety Services, which triggers vehicle emergency stop. Document this handoff explicitly in the interface specification.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual-path with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 availability). The logic is sound: redundancy + automatic failover is a proven approach to achieve high availability in safety-critical systems. DP-B is not over-constrained (allows flexibility in spectrum choice, failover thresholds) and is not under-constrained (specifies dual paths, automatic failover, link quality monitoring). Coupling check passes: failover is transparent to operator and vehicle control logic.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If backup link provisioning is not clearly owned by Network Link module, Teleoperation may be forced to manage infrastructure details (e.g., satellite service contracts), violating separation of concerns.
Diffusion risk: If vehicle firmware must implement link loss watchdog independently, link monitoring logic may be duplicated across Teleoperation and Vehicle modules.

Diffusion risk: If tele-operator console is developed by a separate team without clear Telemetry interface contract, link quality metrics may not be properly displayed, reducing operator situational awareness.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the clear owner (containment_score 0.95). All sub-FRs map to Teleoperation responsibilities. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimize diffusion. Ownership is unambiguous: Teleoperation owns link availability logic; Vehicle owns vehicle control; Safety Services owns safety policy; Network Link owner manages infrastructure.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible and measurable. Link availability can be monitored continuously during evaluation period; uptime/downtime logs can be analyzed to compute availability ratio. Acceptance criteria (≥0.999) is quantitative and testable. Tele-operator and Site Manager can independently verify results.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear. FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (010.1-010.5) are allocated to Teleoperation (command uplink, telemetry downlink, failsafe detection, link quality metrics) and Network Link owner (redundant paths). Safety Services is allocated responsibility for failsafe action (emergency stop) triggered by SafetyAlert signal from Teleoperation.

recommended_revisions: Establish a formal interface contract for Network Link, specifying: (1) dual-path provisioning requirements, (2) failover latency budget (≤100 ms), (3) link quality metrics to be reported (RSRP, latency, packet loss), (4) availability target (≥0.999).

Define SafetyAlert interface in detail: specify the signal format for link loss alert, the expected response time from Safety Services (e.g., emergency stop within 100 ms), and the handoff protocol between Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Conduct a cost-benefit analysis comparing DP-B (dual-path) vs. DP-C (mesh) to support customer decision. Include infrastructure cost, operational complexity, and availability projections for each option.
Establish a design review gate with Safety Manager to verify that failover logic (Teleoperation) and failsafe action (Safety Services) are synchronized and meet safety requirements.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into five sub-FRs (010.1-010.5) that address distinct concerns: command uplink, telemetry downlink, failsafe detection, link quality metrics, and redundant paths. Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. DP-B (dual-path with failover) is sound at this level but must be decomposed into lower-level DPs for each sub-FR (e.g., DP for command uplink protocol, DP for failover decision logic, DP for link quality monitoring). IM assignment to Teleoperation is clear and passes containment analysis, but interface contracts (Network Link, SafetyAlert) must be refined in the next level. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is justified to detail the failover logic, link quality metrics, and safety handoff.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ 500 ms and packet delivery ratio ≥ 99%.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ 500 ms and packet delivery ratio ≥ 99%.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss (primary path unavailable for ≥ 1 second) and initiate failover to backup path within 100 ms.

FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall continuously monitor and report link quality metrics (RSRP, latency, packet loss, link status) to the tele-operator console and Safety Services via Telemetry interface.
FR-NGHE-010.5: The network infrastructure shall provision and maintain dual independent communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover capability.

assumptions: Two independent communication paths (different spectrum/providers) are available at the worksite.
Automatic failover latency (< 100 ms) is achievable with selected network technologies (e.g., LTE + private radio).
Tele-operator console and vehicle firmware can consume link quality metrics via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces without modification.
Safety Services module is capable of receiving SafetyAlert signal and triggering emergency stop within 100 ms.
Evaluation period for availability measurement is at least 30 days to ensure statistical significance.
open_risks: Backup link may have higher latency or lower bandwidth than primary; operator experience may degrade during failover.
Dual-path infrastructure cost may exceed budget; customer may demand cost-benefit trade-off analysis.
Regulatory approval for dual spectrum use (licensed + private) may introduce delays.
If vehicle firmware implements independent link loss watchdog, synchronization with Teleoperation failover logic may be difficult.
Tele-operator console may not properly display link quality metrics if Telemetry interface contract is not clearly defined.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure design document specifying dual-path provisioning, failover latency, and link quality metrics.
SafetyAlert interface specification detailing link loss signal format and expected response time from Safety Services.
Cost-benefit analysis comparing DP-B (dual-path) vs. DP-C (mesh) for customer decision.
Design review gate results confirming synchronization between Teleoperation failover logic and Safety Services failsafe action.
Availability measurement plan and acceptance criteria for evaluation period.
Regulatory approval documentation for dual spectrum use (if applicable).
